questions.question: Why does calling model.eval() before inference in PyTorch change the behavior of Dropout and Batch Normalization?
options: model.eval() switches to a faster inference mode that skips gradient computation.

model.eval() disables Dropout (all neurons are active, scaled by dropout rate) and switches BatchNorm from using mini-batch statistics to using running statistics accumulated during training. This is critical because Dropout during inference would make predictions stochastic, and using mini-batch statistics for single-sample inference would be noisy and incorrect.
model.eval() converts the model to float16 precision for faster inference.
model.eval() enables torch.no_grad() implicitly, preventing gradient computation.
questions.answer: model.eval() disables Dropout (all neurons are active, scaled by dropout rate) and switches BatchNorm from using mini-batch statistics to using running statistics accumulated during training. This is critical because Dropout during inference would make predictions stochastic, and using mini-batch statistics for single-sample inference would be noisy and incorrect.
questions.explanation: Forgetting model.eval() is a very common production bug. A model that 'worked in development' produces noisy, inconsistent predictions in production because Dropout is randomly zeroing neurons on every call. Always use model.eval() + torch.no_grad() when serving predictions.

questions.question: What does gradient clipping (torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_) prevent, and what symptom indicates you need it?
options: Gradient clipping prevents underfitting by ensuring gradients are large enough to train.

Gradient clipping prevents exploding gradients by scaling down gradient vectors that exceed a maximum norm. The symptom is training loss that suddenly spikes to infinity (NaN loss) or oscillates wildly — indicating gradients became so large they pushed weights to extreme values that destabilize the network.
Gradient clipping prevents vanishing gradients in deep networks.
Gradient clipping prevents overfitting by limiting how much weights can change per step.
questions.answer: Gradient clipping prevents exploding gradients by scaling down gradient vectors that exceed a maximum norm. The symptom is training loss that suddenly spikes to infinity (NaN loss) or oscillates wildly — indicating gradients became so large they pushed weights to extreme values that destabilize the network.
questions.explanation: RNNs and Transformers with deep sequences are especially prone to exploding gradients. max_norm=1.0 is the most common value. If you see 'Loss = nan' in your training log, check for exploding gradients first.

questions.question: Why use He (Kaiming) initialization for ReLU networks and Xavier (Glorot) initialization for sigmoid/tanh networks rather than initializing all weights to the same small random value?
options: Named initializers are required by PyTorch and cannot be replaced with custom schemes.

Different activation functions have different variance-preserving requirements. ReLU sets ~50% of activations to zero, effectively halving the variance at each layer — He init compensates with var = 2/fan_in. Sigmoid/tanh saturate at extremes; Xavier init (var = 2/(fan_in+fan_out)) prevents activations from entering saturation regions. Naive uniform initialization causes systematic vanishing or exploding gradients from the very first forward pass.
He and Xavier initialization are only relevant for the final classification layer.
These initializations prevent weight symmetry for the first epoch only.
questions.answer: Different activation functions have different variance-preserving requirements. ReLU sets ~50% of activations to zero, effectively halving the variance at each layer — He init compensates with var = 2/fan_in. Sigmoid/tanh saturate at extremes; Xavier init (var = 2/(fan_in+fan_out)) prevents activations from entering saturation regions. Naive uniform initialization causes systematic vanishing or exploding gradients from the very first forward pass.
questions.explanation: A 50-layer network with ReLU + constant small weights: by layer 50, activations shrink to practically zero — the model learns nothing. He init ensures each layer's output has roughly the same variance as its input, keeping signal alive throughout all layers.
